So the idea here is that we could incorporate some extra information at decoding time using some other distributions, some other data or in this case some other model.
So if you've ever played around with a really like relatively small language model,
maybe something like GBT2 small.
You probably notice you try to give it some inputs and maybe it degenerates into just repeating the same sequence over and over.
Maybe it gives you outputs that are completely incorrect.
If you ask a factual question and it gets it wrong.
And you don't see those problems if you look at sort of a larger model, this train on more data.
So the question here is, can you use what that smaller model is getting wrong to make your larger model even better?
And the way we do this is by sort of the intuition that if the smaller model doesn't have a lot of probability on some answer, but the larger model does.
It's likely because that larger model has learned something that the smaller model didn't know.
And so here we modify the probability distribution coming out of the larger model to choose outputs that that model things are very likely.
And the amateur or the weaker model things are not likely.
So in this example here from their paper, if you have sort of a input life Barack Obama was born in Hawaii, he was born in life.
The smaller model would often do something like start repeating and actually if you sample sort of naive leaf from the larger model, you can wind up in these situations as well.
So if you just choose the most likely thing at each step, you wind up in this loop where it's like he was born in Hawaii, he's born in Hawaii.
And this is behavior in we generally don't want.
If you do something like nucleus or top piece sampling, you can mind up things that are actually completely impressed that she was born in Washington DC.
But if you use contrastive decoding, you take the outputs coming out of your expert model here, and you subtract out the probabilities coming out of the weaker model.
